fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need to ship the async cache library this sprint. the core idea is pretty straightforward — devs should be able to store and fetch values by key, group them into separate logical buckets so they don't step on each other, and optionally have entries disappear after a while without manual cleanup. we talked about this back when we did the storage adapter work, so please keep the same patterns we settled on there.

couple of pain points driving this: support keeps getting tickets about stale data showing up in the UI long after it should be gone, and another team had a nasty bug where two features were accidentally sharing cache keys and corrupting each other's data. we need both of those solved cleanly.

also, the data team flagged that sometimes they need to store non-string stuff — objects, raw binary blobs, booleans, nulls — and right now everything gets mangled. we need to preserve those shapes faithfully on the way out.

bonus: some power users want to plug in their own encode/decode logic around the storage layer, and a few teams want file-backed persistence instead of in-memory only. i'm not 100% sure how the file URI thing should work exactly, just that it needs to feel like a drop-in swap, same as last time with the login module backend swap.

please make sure the internal structure doesn't turn into a god-file situation — last time that bit us hard. keep things clean and separated.

one extra pass on the expiration behavior since a couple folks asked: if a save comes in with lifetime_ms of 0, that means the entry should NEVER expire, even if the cache itself was created with a default_lifetime_ms. that’s different from leaving the lifetime off entirely, which should fall back to the default if there is one. and if there’s no default set and no per-entry lifetime is provided, that entry also never expires.

also wanted to be really explicit about absence vs real values. if we read a key that was never written, or was removed, or already expired, the value we output should be the string 'undefined', like 'foo=undefined'. that sentinel is how we represent “not there.” a real stored null is different and needs to come back as 'null'. same deal for clear: it should print its label with 'undefined' as the value, like 'clear_result=undefined'. no count, no true/false, just undefined every time no matter how much got wiped.

on the namespace side, multiple cache instances can point at the same underlying store object, so isolation has to come from prefixing every stored key with the namespace string, like 'alpha:foo' vs 'beta:foo'. because of that, remove and clear in one namespace can’t touch anything belonging to another one.

for binary data, the input format is JSON objects with a '$bytes_base64' key holding the base64 payload, for example {"$bytes_base64": "YmFy"}. when we print that back out, it should be rendered as 'bytes:<base64string>', for example 'bytes:YmFy'. if that kind of bytes value shows up nested inside a plain object, the object should still be JSON-serialized, just with the nested bytes turned into those 'bytes:<base64string>' strings.

and on the pluggable backend piece, this should follow the same storage adapter abstraction pattern we used before: a backend URI, like a file path, gets passed to the cache constructor as a drop-in replacement for the default in-memory store, with the same public interface and a swappable backend.

trigger_keywords: how does the custom codec hook work
what interface does an encoder decoder need to implement
how do we count encode and decode calls
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A custom codec is an object with an encode function and a decode function. encode is called once per save, decode is called once per successful read (i.e., only when the key exists and has not expired). After the normal output lines, the adapter prints 'encoder_calls=<N>' and 'decoder_calls=<N>' to report how many times each hook was invoked during the scenario.

trigger_keywords: how does the raw record view work
what fields does read_record return
how is expiry metadata exposed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A read_record action returns the stored record shape, not just the value. The output must print two lines using dot notation: '<label>.value=<serialized_value>' and '<label>.expires=<expiry_or_null>'. If the entry was saved without an expiration, expires must render as 'null'.

trigger_keywords: how does file-backed storage work
what does backend temporary_file mean
how is URI-backed persistence configured
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the input specifies 'backend: temporary_file', the adapter must create a real temporary file on disk, instantiate the cache using that file's path as a URI storage backend, and run the scenario against it. The public cache interface (read, save, remove, clear) must behave identically to the in-memory backend from the caller's perspective — same output format, same absence semantics, same clear behavior.

trigger_keywords: how does store_entries work
what does store_entries count
what number is printed for remaining keys after expiry
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The store_entries action reports the number of non-expired entries currently held in the cache's internal store for that namespace. Expired entries that have not yet been actively pruned must NOT be counted — the count must reflect only live entries. The output format is '<label>=<integer_count>'.

trigger_keywords: how should the codebase be structured
should everything go in one file
what is the expected directory layout
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must NOT be a single monolithic file. It requires a multi-file structure (e.g., src/ directory) with clear separation between: the core cache domain logic (store, expiry, namespace), the value serialization/codec layer, the I/O adapter that reads JSON commands and maps them to domain calls, and the output formatter. Tests belong under a separate rcb_tests/ or tests/ directory.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: same as last time with the login module backend swap
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Refers to the 'shared_store: true' input pattern in feature5_namespace_isolation.json, where a single store instance is passed to multiple cache namespaces — the architectural pattern of injecting a shared store rather than creating isolated stores per namespace.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can confirm off the top of my head — you'll want to dig into the existing test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and cross-reference the PRD in start.md. The behavior contract is fully specified there; I'd trust those sources over anything I say from memory.

feature1_basic_storage.json.description: Values written under a logical key can be read back from the same cache namespace, and each successful write reports that the value was accepted.

feature2_removal_and_absence.json.description: A missing key reads as an undefined value; removing an existing key reports success and makes later reads undefined, while removing a missing key reports no removal.

feature3_clear_all_entries.json.description: Clearing a cache namespace removes every value in that namespace and reports an undefined result for the clearing operation.

feature4_expiration_rules.json.description: Stored values can expire after a default lifetime, per-entry lifetimes override the default, and an explicit zero lifetime keeps a value from expiring.

cases.expected_output:
saved=true
saved=true
saved=true
t0_foo=bar
t0_fizz=buzz
t0_ping=pong
t150_foo=bar
t150_fizz=undefined
t150_ping=pong
t250_foo=undefined
t250_ping=pong
t350_ping=undefined

feature5_namespace_isolation.json.description: Independent namespaces can use the same logical keys without collisions; removal or clearing in one namespace does not remove values in another namespace.

cases.expected_output:
saved=true
saved=true
saved=true
saved=true
clear_alpha=undefined
alpha_foo=undefined
alpha_bar=undefined
beta_foo=value-beta
beta_bar=value-beta

feature6_value_shapes.json.description: The cache preserves supported value shapes including booleans, nulls, numbers, objects, byte sequences, quoted strings, and large numeric values.

cases.expected_output:
false_value=false
null_value=null
number_value=0
object_value={"fizz":"buzz"}
bytes_value=bytes:YmFy
object_bytes_value={"buff":"bytes:YnV6eg=="}
quote_value="
large_number_value=9223372036854776000

feature7_raw_record_view.json.description: A caller may request the stored record shape instead of only the value, receiving the saved value together with its expiration metadata.

feature8_custom_codec.json.description: When custom encoding and decoding hooks are supplied, writes pass through the encoder and reads pass through the decoder while preserving the user-facing value.

feature9_uri_backed_storage.json.description: A file-backed storage URI can be used as a cache backend; values can be read after writing and are absent again after the namespace is cleared.
